front: Novel: the protagonist hides her pregnancy to wear a golden bridal wreath, a privilege reserved for virgin brides, at her wedding to Erlend Nikulausson
Novel: the protagonist violates custom by naming one of her eight sons after her still-living husband
Novel: the protagonist breaks off her betrothal to Simon Darre, who ends up marrying her sister Ramborg
Novel: Erlend is killed in a skirmish by a spear to the groin while defending the protagonist from charges of adultery
Novel: the protagonist loses the farm Husaby because her husband plotted to overthrow the king
Novel: the protagonist is sent to Nonneseter convent on account of a rumor involving her foster-brother and the attempted rapist Bentein
Novel: the protagonist dies of the Black Plague in a convent after returning to tend to the sick
Novel: Charles Archer's original English translation cut modernist and stream-of-consciousness passages; Tiina Nunnally's translation restored them
Novel: tetralogy centering on Olav and Ingunn at the title estate; volumes include The Axe and The Snake Pit
Novel: Olav kills his wife's lover, puts the body in a cabin, and burns it down; he cannot confess his guilt
Novel: two characters are betrothed as children, possibly as a joke, which they take seriously
Novel: the title artist in Rome is impregnated by Gert, the father of her former fiance Helge Gram; she kills herself after Helge rapes her
Author: a controversial early novel by this author begins with the line 'I have been unfaithful to my husband'
Author: this Norwegian Nobel laureate was born in Denmark; was a prominent anti-Nazi
